engagement.justification: Query asks for a counterexample to a consistency-via-model definition in logic; the response instead argues the Kantian don't-treat-persons-as-things point, missing the logical criterion entirely.

coherence.justification: The means-vs-ends argument is mostly followable across a first/next/finally structure, with only a few rough clauses.

substance.justification: Develops a coherent ends-not-means line but it is unrelated to the consistency definition asked about, so it does not push the actual question.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Largely native UGF rendering of a Kantian idea; minor clunk ('act toward a people').

engagement.justification: Query is a predictive cross-jurisdictional analogy; the response is a broken stream about a teacher, a church figure, and a boy that engages nothing.

coherence.justification: Run-on fragments with no thread ('he does not get angry by his help from a teacher who knows very well').

substance.justification: No content.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Restriction yields incoherence.

engagement.justification: Query asks about deep lasting happiness; the response is a one-line fragment asserting lasting good points make a lasting happy feeling.

coherence.justification: Readable but starts mid-thought ('but long') and is barely a sentence.

substance.justification: Pure restatement, no development.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF is fine but the content is negligible.

engagement.justification: Beyond a broken opening, engages the correlation-vs-causation/third-cause theme relevant to an argumentative analogy, using the black-stick-and-rain case.

coherence.justification: Followable in stretches but several examples are jumbled ('The gold is gone and the man gets happy', 'made the sun come up and the bird make the noise') causing real confusion.

substance.justification: Pushes the genuine point that co-occurring events may share a hidden common cause and warns against inferring causation from co-occurrence.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF is mostly present but the muddled examples make it clunky and only partly recoverable.

engagement.justification: Query is a defeasible indicative-conditional item; the response develops the justified-true-belief analysis and a Gettier-style lucky-guess case, engaging the underlying knowledge-vs-belief issue if not the conditional form.

coherence.justification: Three-part structure (belief, truth, good reason) plus the game example is followable, though a few clauses break ('did they really know it? No, they just guessed... his reason was still wrong').

substance.justification: Pushes a real distinction with a lucky-correct-guess case showing true justified-seeming belief can still fail to be knowledge.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF carries the epistemology well; a couple of stray punctuation artifacts but recoverable.

engagement.justification: Query is a hypothetical-syllogism item in games/sport; the response argues the persons-are-not-things (Kantian) point, off the conditional-logic ask though a coherent ethical discussion.

coherence.justification: The thing-vs-person contrast runs across a clear first/second/third structure with only minor breaks ('It stops wanting to be something else').

substance.justification: Develops the means/ends idea but it is unrelated to the logical-form prompt, so it does not push the actual question.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Largely native UGF rendering of the hammer/metal analogy and the moral point.

engagement.justification: Query is a constructive-dilemma item in deontology; the one-line response about your mind staying around at sundown engages nothing.

coherence.justification: A single broken clause with no thread.

substance.justification: No content.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF words but no argument.

engagement.justification: Query asks for a counterexample to a consistency definition; the response gropes at 'the set of three things' and what gives 'the first part' but never forms a criterion or counterexample.

coherence.justification: Largely unparseable ('the one that can not happen gives us the first thing; and the two of the one give us the first part').

substance.justification: Hints at set membership but develops nothing.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Restriction produces near-gibberish around the set-talk.

engagement.justification: Query asks why character matters more than following rules; the response is a fragment questioning 'what kind of person who thinks does this idea?', barely touching the theme.

coherence.justification: Starts mid-thought and is too broken to follow.

substance.justification: No case or distinction.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Recognizably UGF but does little work.

engagement.justification: Query asks why one person's story does not generalize; the response only says 'our minds can do things that are wrong', glancing the fallibility theme but not the hasty-generalization point.

coherence.justification: The single sentence is readable but says almost nothing.

substance.justification: Pure truism, no development.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Clean UGF but vacuous.

engagement.justification: Query asks for a counterexample to a necessary-truth definition; the response is a fragment about a 'name' being right that engages nothing.

coherence.justification: Disconnected fragments ('of known, and your name is right').

substance.justification: No content.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Restriction yields incoherence.

coherence.justification: Disjointed and questioning with no thread.

substance.justification: No case or distinction.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF tokens are clean but assemble into nothing.

engagement.justification: Query asks for a counterexample to a resemblance theory of pictorial representation; the response talks about a person feeling bad about money/right-and-wrong, brushing an is/ought-style gap but not depiction.

coherence.justification: Broken and hard to parse ('his words he used do not show that the thing he says about how much money you must give for food are good').

substance.justification: Faintly gestures at a fact/value mismatch but develops no counterexample.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Clunky UGF, only partly recoverable.

engagement.justification: Query asks for a counterexample to a dispositional-belief biconditional; the response is a muddled exchange about whether 'white' can be eaten, not engaging dispositions.

substance.justification: Possibly probes a use/mention or category confusion but it never lands.

engagement.justification: Query asks for a counterexample to a naming success-criterion; the response is a fragmentary dialogue about the 'first part of the god' and spirit, only faintly near naming/reference.

coherence.justification: Disjointed Q&A fragments with no followable argument.

substance.justification: Hints at a reference puzzle but develops nothing.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF words but no recoverable case.

engagement.justification: Directly engages the one-to-help-many problem, framing the clash between aggregate-good and don't-harm-the-innocent reasoning around an explicit sacrifice case.

coherence.justification: Mostly coherent contrast of two views, with a slightly garbled close ('things that seem only possible are true').

substance.justification: Pushes the genuine consequentialist-vs-deontological tension and names the using-a-person-as-a-thing objection.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads natively across an abstract ethical dilemma.

engagement.justification: Query is a predictive geometric analogy; the response is a fragmentary reciprocity-and-fear dialogue that engages nothing geometric or analogical.

coherence.justification: Broken dialogue with no thread.

substance.justification: No content.

coherence.justification: Self-contradictory fragment ('a morning that is night comes').

substance.justification: No content.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF words but no recoverable meaning.

engagement.justification: Engages the ad hominem theme fitting a consequentialist conditional prompt, arguing a claim's truth is independent of the speaker's character with the two-and-two-make-four case.

coherence.justification: Mostly coherent (judge the claim, not the face), though one passage muddles ('a very good person can find things that are true but change their minds').

substance.justification: Pushes the genuine point that a bad person can state truths and a good person can err, separating credibility from truth.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF feels native across the reasoning.

engagement.justification: Engages what a good life via good character means, surveying outward-helping and inward-integrity conceptions of the good life.

coherence.justification: Mostly coherent two-view survey, though a few clauses slip ('they would not want to be wrong to yourself') and the close trails off.

substance.justification: Pushes a real contrast between other-regarding and self-regarding accounts of the good life and notes the tension between them.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads natively across an abstract value discussion.

engagement.justification: Query is a modus-ponens evidential item; the response is a broken fragment about what we know and how the body works, engaging nothing.

coherence.justification: Disjointed clauses with no thread.

substance.justification: No content.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF words but no argument.

engagement.justification: Query asks for a counterexample to a same-cardinality (bijection) definition; the response mumbles about a set pointing to nothing and the sun not being bright on its own, not engaging cardinality.

coherence.justification: Fragmentary and questioning with no thread.

substance.justification: Faint gesture at the empty set but no counterexample.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Restriction yields near-gibberish around the set-talk.

engagement.justification: Query is a literal-similarity analogy in ethics thought-experiments; the response is a word-jumble about a good one and a bad one engaging nothing.

engagement.justification: Despite a broken opening about identity, the body develops correlation-vs-causation and a hidden common cause, relevant to a counterexample about distinctness/over-throw though not the revolution definition itself.

coherence.justification: Followable in stretches but the rain/umbrella example is muddled and several clauses break ('the clouds and the air in the book of the person').

substance.justification: Pushes the genuine point that co-occurring events can share a third cause and that intervention testing is needed, even if it doesn't address revolutions.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF present but clunky; the muddled examples need re-reading.

engagement.justification: Engages deep vs momentary happiness, contrasting fleeting pleasures (money, fun) with a durable well-being that is part of who one is.

coherence.justification: Mostly coherent, though the opening 'can be shown by numbers' clause is dropped rather than developed.

substance.justification: Identifies the lasting-vs-transient distinction and gives examples but does not push it into a sharper claim.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Largely native UGF; one clunky measurement clause.

engagement.justification: Query asks about deep lasting happiness; the response oddly reframes it as unimportant for group life and asks what stays under varied behavior, sidestepping the ask.

coherence.justification: Short and readable but its point is unclear and underdeveloped.

substance.justification: Offers no developed account of deep happiness.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Clean UGF but thin and off-target.

engagement.justification: Query is a literal-similarity analogy in philosophy of mind; the response is an opaque fragment listing 'one for being careful... two for being open' engaging nothing.

coherence.justification: Unparseable ('is, but It. The a of not knowing starts in different things').

substance.justification: No content.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Restriction breaks everything.

engagement.justification: Directly engages why popularity does not make a belief true (argumentum ad populum), stating the thesis and unpacking it.

coherence.justification: Clean first/second/third structure where each step follows; no contradictions.

substance.justification: Pushes the point with the flat-earth historical case and mechanisms (repetition, unchecked confidence) and ends with a clear method, though the moves are fairly standard.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF feels native across the argument.

engagement.justification: Query asks why some deals hold and others fall apart; the response gestures at groups not breaking when shared use binds them and letting go of 'bad stuff', touching cooperation stability but garbled.

coherence.justification: Single tangled sentence ('groups can not break when the only use for things they want are what they do together') is hard to follow.

substance.justification: Faint gesture at incentive-alignment but develops nothing.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF is present but the syntax is clunky and only partly recoverable.

engagement.justification: Directly engages the one-to-help-many problem, weighing aggregate-good against the wrongness of harming the innocent and the using-as-a-thing worry.

coherence.justification: Mostly coherent two-sided treatment, though a couple of clauses are confusingly phrased ('even if that person did nothing bad') and the close trails off.

substance.justification: Pushes both the maximizing rationale and the deontological objection, and raises the felt residue of treating a person as a means.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Largely native UGF; a few clunky seams but the argument holds.
